medications.indication_rationale: Regular simple analgesia is the mainstay of viral pharyngitis — controlling pain is what lets the patient resume drinking and avoid dehydration. A soluble/dispersible formulation is preferable given a fear of swallowing tablets.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg orally every 4-6 hours as needed, maximum 4 g in 24 hours

medications.indication_rationale: An NSAID alternated with paracetamol gives superior throat-pain control; taking analgesia ~30 minutes before attempting fluids links pain relief to drinking. Take with food and confirm no NSAID contraindication (renal impairment, active dehydration risk, GI ulcer, asthma sensitivity) on review.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6-8 hours as needed, with food, maximum 1200 mg in 24 hours for OTC use
contraindications_in_this_case: Use cautiously while dehydrated — ensure fluid intake is being restored; avoid if significant renal impairment or dehydration persists

medications.drug_name: benzydamine hydrochloride 0.15% oromucosal spray or gargle
medications.indication_rationale: Topical benzydamine provides localised throat pain relief and is useful when swallowing tablets is difficult; an adjunct to systemic analgesia, not a substitute.
medications.dose_route_frequency: benzydamine 0.15% spray or gargle to the throat every 1.5-3 hours as needed per product label

medications.indication_rationale: Antibiotics have NO role in viral pharyngitis. The viral constellation (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis) makes bacterial infection unlikely, and prescribing an antibiotic here is an error of commission — no benefit, avoidable harm and resistance. Amoxicillin is additionally hazardous if the sore throat were glandular fever (widespread rash).

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take regular analgesia (paracetamol, and ibuprofen if suitable) about 30 minutes before attempting to drink, so pain relief coincides with drinking.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The barrier to hydration is pain on swallowing; timing analgesia to the functional task of drinking restores oral intake and prevents an admission for IV fluids.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Sip cold fluids and suck ice chips frequently in small amounts; avoid acidic drinks like orange juice that irritate the throat.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cold provides a local numbing effect and small frequent sips are more achievable than large volumes; maintaining hydration is the primary goal.

patient_education_points.point: This is a viral throat infection. It is not helped by antibiotics — the aim is good pain relief so you can keep drinking.
Throat pain from this usually peaks around days 3-5 and then eases over the following week; the cough and tiredness can linger up to about two weeks.
The most important thing right now is keeping fluids up. Use pain relief, then take small frequent cold sips or ice chips.
Because you live alone, check in with someone you trust today so they know you're unwell, and keep your phone nearby.

follow_up_plan.interval: Review with your regular GP within about 3 days (sooner — same day — if you cannot keep fluids down despite pain relief, or if the fever climbs)
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Whether oral hydration has been re-established, pain control, resolution of symptoms, and any development of red-flag or suppurative features
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Unable to maintain any oral fluids despite analgesia, worsening or one-sided throat swelling, difficulty opening the mouth, drooling, voice change, noisy breathing, or feeling faint on standing

must_include_items: Screen the patient-reportable suppurative/airway red flags (trismus, drooling, muffled voice, unilateral swelling, noisy breathing) before reassuring
Recommend an analgesia-first hydration strategy (regular simple analgesia, soluble/topical options given difficulty swallowing tablets)
Advise small frequent cold sips / ice chips to maintain hydration
Explicitly state that antibiotics are not indicated for a viral sore throat
Give clear safety-netting with a same-day in-person escalation if hydration cannot be restored or red flags appear
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending paracetamol alone if there is a reason to avoid an NSAID
Topical benzydamine as the initial analgesic given tablet-swallowing difficulty
Advising a same-day in-person or telehealth GP review proactively given the current inability to drink and living alone
errors_of_omission: Not screening for the suppurative/airway red flags before reassuring a severe sore throat
Not safety-netting the inability to maintain oral intake
errors_of_commission: Prescribing or recommending antibiotics for a viral pharyngitis
Over-triaging a benign self-limiting sore throat to emergency department or an ambulance (000) when no red flags are present
Recommending a throat swab / strep test and antibiotics despite Centor-negative viral features
Inventing a paediatric or weight-based dose (not applicable — adult, and no paediatric dosing permitted)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (1) an analgesia-first hydration self-care plan, (2) an explicit no-antibiotics message, and (3) red-flag safety-netting with a conditional in-person escalation. Over-triaging to ED/000 with no red flags, or prescribing antibiotics, is a failing response even if other content is present. This is a LOW-tier case: correct self-care with safety-netting is the intended passing answer.
